coredata.dc:title: Sensitive detection and intracellular imaging of free copper ions based on DNA-templated silver nanoclusters aggregation-inducing fluorescence enhancement effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Zhiqiang
Lv, Wenchao
Yang, Chen
Ping, Meiling
Fu, FengFu
coredata.dc:description: Free copper ions (Cu+ and Cu2+) have critical toxicity to cells, although copper is an essential element for human body. Hence, sensitive monitoring is crucial to avoid over intake of Cu+/Cu2+. We herein designed a ssDNA sequence (A31) for synthetizing A31-templated silver nanoclusters (AgNCs), and demonstrated that Cu+/Cu2+ can induce the aggregation of A31-templated AgNCs and thus greatly enhanced the fluorescence emission of A31-templated AgNCs. Based on Cu+/Cu2+-induced fluorescence enhancement effect of A31-templated AgNCs, a label-free and signal-on fluorescent sensing platform was developed for the specific and sensitive detection of Cu+/Cu2+ in biological samples and intracellular imaging of Cu+/Cu2+ in cells. The signal-on fluorescent sensing platform could be used to rapidly detect Cu+ and Cu2+ with a detection limit of 0.1 µM within 30 min., and to perform the intracellular imaging of Cu+ and Cu2+ in cells with good cell permeability and biocompatibility. By using the signal-on fluorescent sensing platform, we have successfully detected Cu+ and Cu2+ in cells fluids and human serum with a recovery of 90–104% and a RSD (n = 5) < 5%, and performed the imaging of Cu+/Cu2+ in Hela cells. The developed fluorescent sensing platform has obvious analytical and imaging advantages such as signal-on, simple operation, short analysis time, both Cu+ and Cu2+ detection, similar or higher sensitivity, good cell permeability and biocompatibility, which promising a reliable approach for the rapid and on-site detection or imaging of free copper ions in biological samples in clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silver nanoclusters
Copper
Fluorescent biosensor
Fluorescent imaging
Aptamer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent detection of Cu+ and Cu2+ based on A31-templated AgNCs
Fluorescent detection of free copper ions in cells and human serum
Fluorescent intracellular imaging of free copper ions in cells
The principle of signal-on fluorescent sensing platform for the specific detection/imaging of Cu+/Cu2+ based on A31-templated AgNCs
Characterization of label-free and signal-on A31-templated AgNCs-based fluorescent sensing platform
Conditions optimization of A31-templated AgNCs-based fluorescent sensing platform
The specificity of the developed sensing platform to free Cu+ and Cu2+
The analytical performance of A31-templated AgNCs-based fluorescent sensing platform
Determination of free copper ions in cell and human serum samples
Confocal fluorescence imaging of free copper ions in Hela cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge National Natural Science Foundation of China (21976029) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence emission spectra (A) and the UV–visible extinction spectra (B) of A31-templated AgNCs in the absence and in the presence of free copper ions (Cu+ or Cu2+). The fluorescence excitation wavelength was 542 nm, and the concentrations of Cu+ and Cu2+ were all 100 µM.

TEM images and XPS analysis of the A31-templated AgNCs observed in the fluorescent sensing platform in the absence of free copper ions (A) and in the presence of free Cu+ or Cu2+ (B). The concentration of Cu+ or Cu2+ was 100 µM.

The fluorescence intensity of the sensing platform at 626 nm for detecting various metallic ions under optimal conditions. The concentrations of Cu+, Cu2+ and Hg2+ were all 100 µM, and the concentrations of other ions were all 1.0 mM.

The fluorescence emission spectra for detecting different concentrations of free Cu+ (A) and Cu2+ (B), and their calibration curves (C). The concentrations of Cu+ and Cu2+ were 0.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10, 15, 20, 50, and 100 µM.

Confocal fluorescence bright field (up) and dark field (down) images for Hela cells after cells incubated with basal medium (a); A31-templated AgNCs + basal medium (b); A31-templated AgNCs + Cu+ + basal medium (c); A31-templated AgNCs + Cu2+ + basal medium (d); A31-templated AgNCs + Cu+ + EDTA + basal medium (e); and A31-templated AgNCs + Cu2+ + EDTA + basal medium (f).
Experimental principle of label-free and signal-on fluorescent sensing platform for specific detection of free copper ions based on A31-templated AgNCs.
Analytical results of free copper ions in cells fluid and human serum.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Concentrations in original cells fluids and human serum.

head.ce:title: Sensitive detection and intracellular imaging of free copper ions based on DNA-templated silver nanoclusters aggregation-inducing fluorescence enhancement effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology of MOE, Fujian Provincial Key Lab of Analysis and Detection for Food Safety, College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian 350116, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology of MOE
Fujian Provincial Key Lab of Analysis and Detection for Food Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Zhangzhou Institute of Technology, Zhangzhou, Fujian 36300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Zhangzhou Institute of Technology, Zhangzhou, Fujian 36300, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Free copper ions (Cu+ and Cu2+) have critical toxicity to cells, although copper is an essential element for human body. Hence, sensitive monitoring is crucial to avoid over intake of Cu+/Cu2+. We herein designed a ssDNA sequence (A31) for synthetizing A31-templated silver nanoclusters (AgNCs), and demonstrated that Cu+/Cu2+ can induce the aggregation of A31-templated AgNCs and thus greatly enhanced the fluorescence emission of A31-templated AgNCs. Based on Cu+/Cu2+-induced fluorescence enhancement effect of A31-templated AgNCs, a label-free and signal-on fluorescent sensing platform was developed for the specific and sensitive detection of Cu+/Cu2+ in biological samples and intracellular imaging of Cu+/Cu2+ in cells. The signal-on fluorescent sensing platform could be used to rapidly detect Cu+ and Cu2+ with a detection limit of 0.1 µM within 30 min., and to perform the intracellular imaging of Cu+ and Cu2+ in cells with good cell permeability and biocompatibility. By using the signal-on fluorescent sensing platform, we have successfully detected Cu+ and Cu2+ in cells fluids and human serum with a recovery of 90–104% and a RSD (n = 5) < 5%, and performed the imaging of Cu+/Cu2+ in Hela cells. The developed fluorescent sensing platform has obvious analytical and imaging advantages such as signal-on, simple operation, short analysis time, both Cu+ and Cu2+ detection, similar or higher sensitivity, good cell permeability and biocompatibility, which promising a reliable approach for the rapid and on-site detection or imaging of free copper ions in biological samples in clinical diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper (Cu) is an essential element for human body, which is responsible for the homeostasis of living organisms in various biological processes [1,2]. However, the free copper ions including Cu+ and Cu2+ have critical toxicity to cells [3,4]. In fact, although the total copper in healthy cells is about 100 µM, the free copper ions (Cu+ and Cu2+) was reported to be less than one free ion per cell averagely [5,6]. It was reported that, once the copper concentration exceeds the body’s need, free copper ions can give rise to serious copper poisoning and neurodegenerative diseases, such as prion disease, Parkinson, Menkes, Wilson, and Alzheimer syndrome [7–9]. Therefore, a limit of 2.0 mg/L in drinking water was set for copper by World Health Organization (WHO), and a stricter copper limit (1.0 mg/L) in drinking water was set by China government [10]. Thus, it is crucial to monitor the concentrations of free copper ions (Cu+ and Cu2+) in biological samples in clinical diagnosis.

In reality, the copper contents in biological samples were mainly determined with instrument-based methods, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES), and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [11,12]. However, the instrument-based methods can only detect the concentrations of total copper, not free copper ions. The limitations of the instrument-based methods pushed researchers to develop various simple methods for sensing or monitoring free copper ions. Along the goal, several colorimetric methods have been developed for the visual detection of free Cu2+ due to the outstanding advantages of colorimetric sensor such as no requirement of sophisticated instrument, portability and so on [13–20]. However, most colorimetric methods previously reported suffered from one or more of following deficiencies, such as lower sensitivity, poorer resistibility to the matrix of sample, complex and time-consuming operation process, no capability of imaging and so on.

Fluorescent method has higher sensitivity, better selectively and is easy to operate, and thus has been widely exploited for the detection or imaging of Cu2+ [21,22]. Especially, various fluorescent nanoparticles including semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), nanoclusters and carbon dots (CDs) have been wildly reported for the detection or imaging of Cu2+ in biological samples due to the advantages of good water dispersibility, good cell permeability and biocompatibility [23–34]. Unfortunately, most previous fluorescent nanoparticles-based methods need a relatively complex and time-consuming process, detect only Cu2+ without free Cu+, and most of them employed signal-off strategy, which is prone to generate false positive results [25–34].

Noble metal nanoclusters have unique fluorescence characteristics. Especially, silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) can be simply synthesized by using an exquisitely-designed ssDNA sequence as template, and the DNA-templated AgNCs have strong fluorescence emission ability, as well as excellent biocompatibility and excellent stability [35–37]. In previous studies, several researchers reported that Cu2+ can quench the fluorescence emission of DNA-templated AgNCs or other AgNCs via electrostatic interaction or energy transfer [38–40], and thus developed AgNCs-based signal-off fluorescent method for the detection of Cu2+ [29,38]. Whereas, in our previous work, we found unexpectedly that free Cu+ or Cu2+ can significantly enhance the fluorescence emission of DNA-templated AgNCs, which using an exquisitely-designed ssDNA sequence as template [41]. Inspired by this phenomenon, we herein developed a simple label-free and signal-on fluorescent sensing platform for the rapid detection and intracellular imaging of Cu+ and Cu2+ in cells by using DNA-templated AgNCs as signal molecules, in the hope of providing a potential convenient method for monitoring free copper ions in biological samples in clinical diagnosis.

The information of reagents including oligonucleotides sequences and apparatus used in the experiments was summarized in Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI). For preparing DNA (A31)-templated AgNCs, firstly, 20 µL of 100 µM DNA template (5′-ACCCGAACCTGGGCTACCACCCTTAATCCCC-3′, sequence A31 in Table S1 in ESI) solution, 20 µL of 1.2 mM AgNO3 solution and 300 µL of 20 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) were mixed in a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube under 5 °C. The whole was incubated for 1.0 h under gentle agitation and room temperature against light exposure. Then, 20 µL of 0.6 mM fresh NaBH4 solution (dissolved in cold water) was added, and the whole was gently mixed for 30 min in the dark at room temperature to synthesize A31-templated AgNCs. The as-prepared A31-templated AgNCs solution was stored at 5 °C and used for detecting free copper ion within 2 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of Cu+ and Cu2+ based on A31-templated AgNCs
ce:section.ce:para: For detecting free Cu+ and Cu2+, 180 µL of as-prepared A31-templated AgNCs solutions was added into a clean micro centrifuge tube, and then 10 µL of fresh Cu+ standard or Cu2+ standard (or sample solution) was added. The whole was gently mixed for 5 min., and then the fluorescence emission spectrum of solution was measured with a microplate reader in the wavelength range of 570–770 nm under excitation of 542 nm. The concentration of Cu+ or Cu2+ was calculated according to the signal gain: ΔF/F0 = (F-F0)/F0, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of A31-templated AgNCs solutions at 626 nm in the presence and absence of Cu+ or Cu2+ respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of free copper ions in cells and human serum
ce:section.ce:para: For detecting Cu+ and Cu2+ in cell fluids, Hela cells were firstly lysed by heat shock (repeated freeze-thawing) to obtain cell fluid [42]. In detail, the digested Hela cells were frozen at −20 °C and thawed at 50 °C for 30 min respectively, and the freeze-thawing cycle was repeated for three times. Then, the cell fluid was separated and collected via centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The obtained cell fluid was diluted 10 times with ultrapure water, and the concentrations of free copper ions (Cu+ and Cu2+) in final solution was detected with above method.

For detecting free copper ions in human serum, firstly, 1.2 mL of acetonitrile was added in 2 mL of human serum to remove serum protein. Then, the serum was further centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the serum protein residues. The supernatant was diluted for 50-fold with ultrapure water and then used for the detection of free copper ions with above method. The cell fluids and human serum spiked with different concentrations of Cu+ and/or Cu2+ were also pre-treated and detected with the same manner to obtain recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent intracellular imaging of free copper ions in cells
ce:section.ce:para: For imaging intracellular free copper ions, firstly, Hela cells were digested with 0.25% Trypsin-0.53 mM EDTA (TE) solution, and then were plated on laser confocal culture dishes for 48 h. After washed twice with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution, the cells were further cultured for 6 h in different culture mediums respectively as follow: (1) 500 µL of cell basal medium; (2) 200 µL of A31-templated AgNCs + 300 µL of cell basal medium; (3) 180 µL of A31-templated AgNCs + 20 µL of Cu+ (100 µM) + 300 µL cell basal medium; (4) 180 µL of A31-templated AgNCs + 20 µL of Cu2+ (100 µM) + 300 µL of cell basal medium; (5) 180 µL of A31-templated AgNCs + 20 µL of Cu+ (100 µM) + 20 µL of EDTA (100 µM) + 280 µL cell basal medium; (6) 180 µL of A31-templated AgNCs + 20 µL of Cu2+ (100 µM) + 20 µL of EDTA (100 µM) + 280 µL cell basal medium. The treated cells were washed twice with PBS, and then were used for fluorescence imaging analysis by using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) with a 488 nm laser excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The principle of signal-on fluorescent sensing platform for the specific detection/imaging of Cu+/Cu2+ based on A31-templated AgNCs
ce:section.ce:para: It was reported cytosine (C) base in oligonucleotides has higher affinity to Ag+ due to the formation of C-Ag+-C structures, and thus an exquisitely-designed C-rich ssDNA sequence can be used as template to synthetize AgNCs (DNA-templated AgNCs) with excellent stability, strong fluorescence emission and good biocompatibility [35]. It was also reported that some metallic ions can effectively aggregate thiolate-protected metal nanoclusters (NCs) via the coordinate bonding between metallic ions and thiolate, and thus enhance the fluorescence emission of thiolate-protected metal NCs [43]. Inspired by above results, we herein developed a label-free and signal-on fluorescent sensing platform for the rapid detection and intracellular imaging of free copper ions (Cu+ and Cu2+) in cells by using DNA-templated AgNCs as receptor. As the Scheme 1 showed, an exquisitely-designed C-rich ssDNA sequence (A31) can bind with Ag+, and then to form A31-templated AgNCs, which emits fluorescence at 626 nm, after the associated Ag+ was reduced by NaBH4. In the presence of free Cu+/Cu2+, the Cu+/Cu2+ could bring the aggregation of DNA template (A31) by interacting with outer phosphate parts of DNA [44], and thus induced the aggregation of A31-templated AgNCs, which greatly enhanced the fluorescence emission of A31-templated AgNCs. In addition, the as-prepared A31-templated AgNCs has excellent water dispersibility, cell permeability and biocompatibility. This provided a simple label-free and signal-on fluorescence sensing platform for the rapid detection and intracellular imagine of Cu+/Cu2+ in biological samples in clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of label-free and signal-on A31-templated AgNCs-based fluorescent sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the feasibility of signal-on A31-templated AgNCs-based fluorescent sensing platform, the fluorescence emission spectra of the A31-templated AgNCs were investigated in the absence and presence of Cu+/Cu2+, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1 -A, under the excitation of 542 nm wavelength, the as-prepared A31-templated AgNCs emitted weaker fluorescence with a maximum wavelength at 626 nm. Upon the addition of Cu+ or Cu2+, the fluorescence emission of A31-templated AgNCs at 626 nm was markedly enhanced. Whereas, in the presence of both EDTA and Cu+ or Cu2+, the fluorescence emission of A31-templated AgNCs at 626 nm do not be enhanced. All above results indicated that only free Cu+ or Cu2+ could markedly enhance the fluorescence emission of A31-templated AgNCs, whereas, complexed Cu+ or Cu2+ could not induce the fluorescence enhancement effect of A31-templated AgNCs.

As we mentioned above, some metallic ions such as Al3+ can aggregate thiolate-protected metal NCs by coordinately bonding to thiolate, and thus enhance the fluorescence emission of thiolate-protected metal NCs [43]. It was also reported that Cu+/Cu2+ could bring the aggregation of C-rich DNA sequences by interacting with outer phosphate parts of DNA [44]. Based on previous reports, the possible mechanism of Cu+/Cu2+-induced fluorescence enhancement effect was inferred as follow: the Cu+/Cu2+ aggregated the C-rich DNA template (A31) by interacting with outer phosphate parts of DNA, and thus bring the aggregation of A31-templated AgNCs, which markedly enhanced the fluorescence emission of A31-templated AgNCs due to aggregation-induced emission enhancement behavior. To verify above inference, the UV–visible extinction spectra of A31-templated AgNCs solution were investigated. As Fig. 1-B showed, the pure A31-templated AgNCs solution is pale-yellow, and has three obvious adsorption peaks at 432 nm, 542 nm and 652 nm respectively, like previously reported [45]. Upon the addition of free Cu+ or Cu2+, the color of A31-templated AgNCs solution changed from pale-yellow to light-purple and the adsorption peak at 652 nm disappeared, whereas the adsorption peaks at 432 nm and 542 nm kept no change (Fig. 1-B). The UV–visible extinction results verified that the Cu+ or Cu2+ only induced the aggregation of A31-templated AgNCs, but no interaction happened between Cu+ or Cu2+ and AgNCs since it was reported that the ground state interaction between Cu+/Cu2+ and AgNCs will results in a significant decrease and red-shift of adsorption peak at 542 nm [45]. The fluorescence signal changes of A31-templated AgNCs were also investigated by adding Cu+/Cu2+ first and then EDTA. The experimental results revealed that the fluorescence emission of A31-templated AgNCs first enhance with the addition of Cu+/Cu2+, and then turn to decrease to initial fluorescence intensity upon the addition of EDTA, which further indicating that Cu+/Cu2+ only induced A31-templated AgNCs to generate aggregation, but do not chemically interact with AgNCs.

The imaging analysis of scanning transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and elemental analysis of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) also supported our above deduction. As the TEM images observed in Fig. 2 , the as-prepared A31-templated AgNCs are quasi-spherical in shape with a size of ∼5 nm and revealed good mono-dispersity (Fig. 2-A), and C, O, N, P and Ag elements were detected in its XPS spectrum (Fig. 2-A and Fig. S1 in ESI), demonstrating the formation of A31-templated AgNCs. Upon the addition of free Cu+ or Cu2+, a ∼ 50 nm particle consisting of dozens of ∼ 5 nm AgNCs was observed (Fig. 2-B), indicating that free Cu+ or Cu2+ induced A31-templated AgNCs to generate aggregation and thus enhanced the fluorescence emission. However, no Cu element was detected in the XPS spectra of aggregated A31-templated AgNCs (Fig. 2-B and Fig. S1 in ESI), indicating that free Cu+ or Cu2+ only play a role to induce the aggregation of A31-templated AgNCs, Cu+ or Cu2+ itself does not be reduced to Cu0 on the surface of A31-templated AgNCs or be adsorbed on the surface of A31-templated AgNCs. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) sizes of A31-templated AgNCs in the absence and presence of Cu+/Cu2+ were also measured with a Nano particles size analyzer (Microtrac Nanotrac Wave II), and results showed (Fig. S2 in ESI) that the original A31-templated AgNCs has an average DLS size of 11.43 nm and the aggregated A31-templated AgNCs induced by Cu+/Cu2+ has an average DLS size of 61.98 nm.

In this study, the ssDNA sequence was not only used as template to synthetize AgNCs, but also used to specially generate free Cu+/Cu2+-induced aggregation of DNA-templated AgNCs. Thus, the selection of ssDNA sequence is one of key factors. By referring to previous literature [35,40], two different ssDNA sequences of A31 and A33 (see Table S1 in ESI), which were widely used as template to synthetize AgNCs, were investigated in order to realize the fluorescent detection of free Cu+ and Cu2+ with high sensitivity and excellent specificity. As Fig. S3 (see ESI) revealed, when ssDNS sequence of A33 was used as template to synthetize A33-templated AgNCs, the fluorescence enhancement effect of A33-templated AgNCs induced by free Cu+ or Cu2+ is low, and the fluorescence enhancement effect induced by Cu+ is different from that induced by free Cu2+. Whereas, when ssDNS sequence of A31 was used as template to synthetize A31-templated AgNCs, the addition of free Cu+ or Cu2+ can lead to the same and biggest fluorescence enhancement effect. In addition, the signal-on fluorescence sensing platform revealed excellent stability when A31 was used as template to synthetize A31-templated AgNCs. The original A31-templated AgNCs retains 90% of the initial fluorescence intensity after it was stored at 5 °C for 2 days, and the aggregated A31-templated AgNCs induced by Cu+/Cu2+ retains 90% of the initial fluorescence intensity after it was stored at 5 °C for 5 days. Thus, the sequence of A31 was selected as the optimal ssDNA sequence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conditions optimization of A31-templated AgNCs-based fluorescent sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of the system will affect the redox potential and the stability of NaBH4 solution, and thus affects the preparation of A31-templated AgNCs. In addition, the pH also affects the binding affinity of A31 sequence to free Cu+/Cu2+, and finally affects the sensitivity and specificity of the sensing platform. From the results in Fig. S4 (see ESI), we can observed that the pH of the system greatly affected the fluorescence emission of the as-prepared A31-templated AgNCs, as well as the fluorescence enhancement effect induced by free Cu+ or Cu2+. When pH is 6.5, the as-prepared A31-templated AgNCs has a weak fluorescence emission and free Cu+/Cu2+ almost cannot induce A31-templated AgNCs to generate fluorescence enhancement effect. In a higher pH system (pH 8.5), the as-prepared A31-templated AgNCs also has a weaker fluorescence emission and free Cu+/Cu2+ can induce A31-templated AgNCs to generate a lower fluorescence enhancement effect. Only under pH 7.0 and 7.5, the as-prepared A31-templated AgNCs has a strong fluorescence emission, and free Cu+/Cu2+ can induce A31-templated AgNCs to generate a maximum fluorescence enhancement effect. All above facts suggested that free Cu+/Cu2+ can be sensitively detected under pH 7.0–7.5. Finally, we selected pH 7.0 was optimal pH in this study.

The concentrations of DNA sequence A31, Ag+ and NaBH4 solution directly affected the effective formation of A31-templated AgNCs, and thus affected the analytical performances of the sensing platform. To obtain the optimal concentration of DNA sequence A31, the A31 concentration was optimized within 50–125 µM under pH 7.0. The experimental results (Fig. S5 in ESI) revealed that assay system exhibited a weaker fluorescence emission and lower Cu+/Cu2+-induced fluorescence enhancement effect when A31 concentration is lower than 100 μM, since no adequate DNA templates was provided for synthetizing adequate A31-templated AgNCs. When the A31 concentration is 100 μM, the as-prepared A31-templated AgNCs has a maximum Cu+/Cu2+-induced fluorescence enhancement effect. Consequently, 100 μM of A31 was selected and used in the experiment. The concentration of Ag+ was optimized in the range of 0.3–1.8 mM, and the experimental results (Fig. S6 in ESI) showed that the as-prepared A31-templated AgNCs has an extremely low fluorescence signal and Cu+/Cu2+-induced fluorescence enhancement effect when Ag+ concentration is lower than 1.2 mM, since no adequate Ag+ was provided for synthetizing enough quantity of A31-templated AgNCs. Whereas, too high Ag+ content (1.8 mM) may prevented the effective formation of A31-templated AgNCs, and thus results in a lower fluorescence signal and bad sensitivity too. When Ag+ concentration is 1.2 mM, the sensing platform has a strong fluorescence emission and a maximum Cu+/Cu2+-induced fluorescence enhancement effect. Therefore, 1.2 mM of Ag+ was selected and used in the experiment. The NaBH4 concentration was optimized in the range of 0.6–2.4 mM under above optimal conditions, the results shown in Fig. S7 (see ESI) revealed, the as-prepared A31-templated AgNCs has higher fluorescence emission and the sensing platform has a maximum Cu+/Cu2+-induced fluorescence enhancement effect when NaBH4 concentration changes within 0.6–1.2 mM. When the NaBH4 concentration is higher than 1.2 mM, the as-prepared A31-templated AgNCs has lower fluorescence emission and the sensing platform has a lower Cu+/Cu2+-induced fluorescence enhancement effect. In this study, 0.6 mM was selected as the optimal NaBH4 concentration and used in experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The specificity of the developed sensing platform to free Cu+ and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Many common metallic ions such as Fe3+, Zn2+, K+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Al3+, Na+, Sr2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Cd2+ and Hg2+ coexisting in biological samples might interfere with the detection of free copper ions (Cu+ and Cu2+). To examine the specificity of the established sensing platform, the above common metallic ions (except Hg2+) mentioned at 1.0 mM, which is 10-fold that of free Cu+/Cu2+, were determined with the developed sensing platform. The results displayed in Fig. 3 revealed, other metallic ions did not affect the fluorescence emission of the A31-templated AgNCs although their concentrations are much higher (10-fold) than free Cu+/Cu2+, and only free Cu+ or Cu2+ can induce A31-templated AgNCs to generate significant fluorescence enhancement effect. In addition, as Fig. 1-A showed, the complexed Cu+ or Cu2+ also could not induce the enhancement effect of fluorescence emission of A31-templated AgNCs. All above facts verified that the developed label-free and signal-on fluorescent sensing platform has good selectivity, and can be used to specifically and sensitively monitor free Cu+ and Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analytical performance of A31-templated AgNCs-based fluorescent sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Under above optimal conditions, a series concentration of free Cu+ and Cu2+ (2.0, 5.0, 10, 15, 20, 50, and 100 µM) was detected with the developed signal-on fluorescent sensing platform, and their fluorescence emission spectra were monitored. The results in Fig. 4 showed, with the increasing of free Cu+ or Cu2+ concentration from 2.0 µM to 100 µM, the maximum fluorescence emission at 626 nm (F626) of assay system increased step by step (Fig. 4-A, B). The signal gain (ΔF/F0) showed good linear relationship with the concentrations of free Cu+ or Cu2+ in the range of 2.0–20 µM, with an equation of ΔF/F0 = 0.0239 × C (µM) − 0.0008 and ΔF/F0 = 0.0236 × C (µM) + 0.0513 for Cu+ and Cu2+ respectively (Fig. 4-C). Results shown in Fig. 4-C confirmed that the developed fluorescent sensing platform has the same sensitivity for Cu+ and Cu2+, and thus can be used to detect the total concentrations of free Cu+ and Cu2+, i.e., the concentration of free copper ions. The theoretical detection limits (3σ/S) were calculated to be 0.10 µM for all Cu+ and Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of free copper ions in cell and human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the applicability of our sensing platform, the Cu+/Cu2+ in the fluids of Hela cells and human serum were detected with the proposed method, and cell fluids and human serum spiked with different concentrations of Cu+ or Cu2+ were also measured to obtain recovery. As the results listed in Table 1 , the content of free Cu+/Cu2+ in Hela cells fluid and human serum can be specifically detected with a recovery of 90% − 104% and a relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 5) ≤ 5%, without the interference of other metallic ions and complexed Cu+ and Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal fluorescence imaging of free copper ions in Hela cells
ce:section.ce:para: Hela cells were selected to verify the viability of our sensing platform for intracellular imaging of free Cu+/Cu2+, by incubating with A31-templated AgNCs. As Fig. 5 exhibited, Hela cells incubating without A31-templated AgNCs showed almost no any fluorescence signal (Fig. 5-a); whereas, Hela cells incubating with only A31-templated AgNCs showed a weak red fluorescence signal (Fig. 5-b), indicating that A31-templated AgNCs successfully internalized into the cells. Hela cells incubating with both A31-templated AgNCs and free Cu+ or Cu2+ showed a much stronger intracellular fluorescence signal (Fig. 5-c, d), illustrating that free Cu+ or Cu2+ in the cells induced the aggregation of A31-templated AgNCs to generation obvious fluorescence enhancement effect. Like Hela cells incubating with only A31-templated AgNCs, Hela cells incubating with all A31-templated AgNCs, EDTA and free Cu+ or Cu2+ also showed weak red fluorescence signal, further verified that complexed Cu+ and Cu2+ in cells could not induce the aggregation of A31-templated AgNCs to generation obvious fluorescence enhancement effect. All above facts suggested that the developed label-free and signal-on fluorescent sensing platform has higher sensitivity, better stability, good cell permeability and biocompatibility, and is reliable for the rapid and specific detection or imaging of free Cu+ and Cu2+ in cells. To perform the intracellular imaging of free Cu+/Cu2+ with A31-templated AgNCs, the cytotoxicity of A31-templated AgNCs is a critical factor. The cytotoxicity of A31-templated AgNCs was evaluated by culturing Hela cells with A31-templated AgNCs and measuring the cell viability (details see section 2 in ESI). As Fig. S8 (see ESI) revealed, Hela cells maintained high cell viability in the presence of A31-templated AgNCs, indicating that A31-templated AgNCs possesses excellent biocompatibility.

So far, the copper contents in biological samples were mainly determined with instrument-based methods [11,12]. However, the instrument-based methods can detect the concentrations of total copper, not free copper ions. Several nanoparticles-based fluorescent methods including AgNCs-based fluorescent methods have been reported for Cu2+ detection or imaging [23–34,38]. However, almost previous methods need a relatively complex and time-consuming process, detect only Cu2+ without free Cu+, and employed signal-off strategy, which is prone to generate false positive results [25–34,38]. In comparison with previous methods, the fluorescent sensing platform reported in this study has obvious analytical advantages such as signal-on, simple operation, short analysis time, detection of both Cu+ and Cu2+, similar or higher sensitivity, and good cell permeability and biocompatibility (Table S2 in SI), which make our method a promising approach for the rapid and on-site detection or imaging of free copper ions in biological samples in clinical diagnosis.

In summary, we herein designed a ssDNA sequence (A31) for synthetizing A31-templated AgNCs, and demonstrated that free Cu+/Cu2+ can induce the aggregation of A31-templated AgNCs and thus greatly enhanced the fluorescence emission of A31-templated AgNCs. Based on Cu+/Cu2+-induced fluorescence enhancement effect of A31-templated AgNCs, a label-free and signal-on fluorescent sensing platform was developed for the specific and sensitive detection or intracellular imaging of free Cu+ and Cu2+ in cells. The signal-on fluorescent sensing platform could be used to rapidly detect Cu+/Cu2+ with a detection limit of 0.10 µM within 30 min., and has been successfully employed to detect free Cu+ and Cu2+ in cells fluids and human serum with a recovery of 90–104% and a RSD (n = 5) < 5%. The signal-on fluorescent sensing platform has also been used to perform the intracellular imaging of free Cu+/Cu2+ in cells with better cell permeability and biocompatibility. The developed fluorescent sensing platform has obvious analytical advantages such as signal-on, simple operation, short analysis time, both Cu+ and Cu2+ detection, similar or higher sensitivity, and better cell permeability and biocompatibility, which promising a reliable approach for the rapid and on-site detection or imaging of trace free copper ions in cells in clinical diagnosis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge National Natural Science Foundation of China (21976029) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121734.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
